text: So here's the brutal truth. About 80% of portfolio projects I see actually hurt your chances of
getting hired. And it's not because you're bad at SQL or Tableau. It's actually just because these
projects make you look more like a student than a data analyst. Over the past few years, I have seen
hundreds of portfolio projects and I've mentored countless students to getting their first jobs
in data at companies like Uber, Grubhub and Pandora. And I've seen exactly what separates good from
great. Now there are usually five fatal mistakes that I see most portfolios commit. So today I'm
going to share what those fatal mistakes are. And then I'm going to give you brutally honest
feedback on your portfolio projects and grade these portfolio projects on three main aspects
that hiring managers are looking for business relevance, analytical rigor, and professional
polish. So by the end of this video, you'll know exactly where you stand. If you're new here,
I'm Christine. I'm a former data director and a hiring manager who now helps people take the
the most direct path to working a job in data. And on this channel, I share the strategies,
frameworks, and the mindset tips to help you stand out. Let's dive in.
So I'm going to order these mistakes from least to most fatal, so you know which ones are the
most important ones to prioritize. Now, the first one is creating a fancy portfolio website. Don't
get me wrong, nice aesthetics and design is great, but hiring managers are never coming to your
portfolio and thinking, whoa, this color palette is so beautiful. We should hire them to analyze
our data. They more so want to see what kinds of insights you can bring to their team on day one.
Now, many people will spend weeks building a beautiful portfolio website, figuring out the
animations, the color scheme, and the fonts, when it actually would be much more effective to use
that time to find some real world opportunities through volunteering or practicing and learning
something like data storytelling. So instead of building a fancy website, just use good old GitHub
to host your projects and make sure to link directly to your best projects readme in your
resume, your cover letter, and your LinkedIn profile so that your best work is just one click away.
Hire managers don't want to scroll through five different tabs of about me and see all the
certifications you've done. We want to be brought to your analytical skill set right away. Now the
second mistake is overcompensating by focusing more on quantity than quality. I have seen portfolios
with five, six, even seven projects that are all half-baked or overly technical or just don't make
you look like an actual data analyst. Having more projects does not make you look more impressive.
It just makes you look unfocused. So instead, you want to make sure that you go deep on one or two
analytics-focused projects. So this means that they should be centered around business metrics
and representative data. They should be focused on executive questions and showcase your ability to
use analytical frameworks as well as data best practices. And 90% of your write-up should be
about your insights and your impact. Now I call this the Ready Framework, which I talk about more
in my portfolio playbook series. So check that out afterwards. One of my students, Danielle,
had done a fancy Kaggle project that had gone nowhere. And when she reframed this project
around marketing metrics and ROI, she was able to build a portfolio that looked much more impressive.
And she ended up landing a job as a data analyst at JAL just a few weeks later. So just remember
that if your portfolio looks like homework, it's going to be graded like homework.
Now, a lot of people actually tank their portfolios by mixing roles. They have one project on machine
learning, another project on a data pipeline, and then another one on a Tableau dashboard.
And all this says to a hiring manager is, I'm not really sure what I want to be. So before you start
on a new project, make sure to be intentional about the kinds of roles that you're applying to.
And remember that data scientists build models, data engineers build pipelines,
data analysts extract insights. So your project titles should actually sound like analysts work.
For example, Spotify retention analysis or e-commerce sales trends or marketing ROI and
budget analysis. You should stay away from titles like machine learning in Python or
ETL data pipeline for sales data or statistical analysis and prediction model for churn.
Those scream data science or data engineering. So make sure to reference real business metrics,
industry names, company names in the titles of your projects so that it's very clear that you're
focused on data analyst roles. The number four is brutal and it's one of the most common and fatal
mistakes I see. About 70% of projects I see end with code as the final deliverable. Now picture
this, let's say you work with a sales manager at work and they've asked you to do some analysis on
last month's sales performance and you come to the next meeting with a 100 line SQL query. Now
that's not analysis and they're going to wonder what the heck they should actually do with this
query. Now you should be gearing your portfolio projects to a non-technical stakeholder because
this shows the real work and insights you will be delivering at work. So this means that your
project should focus on three main things, insights, recommendations, and visualizations
that help with storytelling. So your GitHub repo can still show your code, your data, and your
technical documentation, but you should separate this just into the files in the repository and
leave the readme to be the front page that shows the story of your findings. This means that you
players. There's the files that house all the technical stuff. And then there's a write-up
that shows the work that you would actually be delivering on the job as an analyst. This
means that if someone wants to dive into your technical work, they can, but they immediately
see the kind of value that you bring as an analyst first.
And finally, the worst mistake of all, the passion project that nobody asked for. These
are projects that are grounded in personal interests rather than the actual data and metrics
that you would see at an actual company. So if you're doing analysis on Dungeons and
Dragon data, or you downloaded your Spotify wrapped and are analyzing the trends in your own songs
over the last few years, then that's not showing me that you know how to do analysis. It's just
showing me that you have a hobby. So you can definitely use public data sets, but just make
sure that your projects are framed around a company context. So instead of doing analysis like SQL
queries on Dungeons and Dragons stats, it should be something like video game retailer sales analysis,
or instead of writing SQL queries on the outcomes of the US Open, see if you can do analysis on
marketing platform sales. So something that is related to your personal interest, but still is
very easily applicable to a wide variety of different industries. Now that one shift from
personal to professional framing is going to instantly change how recruiters perceive your
portfolio. So just remember that your portfolio should sound like work that a company would
actually have you do. By the way, if you want me to guide you through this process step by step
